quotes.quote: Rather than posting things scattershot on Twitter, Bluesky, Github, and in private DMs and texts, I'm going to share my thoughts publicly and regularly in one place: Materialized View.
The newsletter will go out like messages in a Kafka producer with a linger.ms of 2 weeks and a batch.size of 3-4 items.
SQLite is showing up more and more in distributed systems. I think this trend will continue.

LiteFS works by interposing a very thin virtual filesystem between your app and your on-disk database file. It's not a file system like ext4, but rather a pass-through. Think of it as a file system proxy.
This works particularly well for SaaS applications where each customer is isolated from one another.
Most people who've worked on SaaS immediately get why this database is a big deal.
With Nile, all of this moves to the database layer. Pretty slick.
Docstore is a general-purpose multi-model database that provides a strict serializability consistency model on a partition level and can scale horizontally to serve high volume workloads.
We've reimagined and implemented a much more advanced version of TCP and called it QUIC. And because we want to make QUIC easier to deploy, we run it over UDP.
Two things always annoyed me about the way I implemented job sizing in Samza: Jobs were defined by physical requirements (memory, cpu, disk, network). Resource requirements were static.
Both are undesirable because it's hard for application developers to guess physical requirements and because resource requirements change over time.
Since 2016, WiscKey's architecture has been widely adopted for LSM stores.
I think of it as a mashup between three different framework styles: Airflow/Prefect/Dagster-style workflows, Durable execution frameworks, Traditional business process modeling and automation.
Viewstamped Replication is a consistency algorithm that predates Paxos and Raft.
Say goodbye to flaky tests! This single-threaded design is the key to the determinism and reproducibility of our randomized testing systems.
Alex recommends reducing variability in both space (IO) and time (clocks).
To swizzle-clog, you first pick a random subset of nodes in the cluster. Then, you 'clog' (stop) each of their network connections one by one over a few seconds.
This pattern seems to be particularly good at finding deep issues that only happen in the rarest real-world cases.
This is harder than it sounds, hence all of this wonderful content.
The current market can't sustain this many startups. For even a few big winners, the market has to become much larger.
The adoption cost for durable execution is too high because the frameworks are too hard to use.
I don't use these frameworks unless I have to.
Many of the common pitfalls that Chris lists in his post are exactly the same problems that make stream processing hard.
Right now, we have a collection of first-generation durable execution and stream processing frameworks. No one has really nailed the hard parts and the APIs are clunky.
That's not a dig on any of these teams; these problems are really, really tough. I have the scars to prove it.
Where a16z sees a database, I see a write-ahead log.
We need more open source transactional key-value stores on S3 with a variety of consensus protocols, APIs, and features.
Serverless databases could use these stores as a building block.
I expected Flink usage to mostly be analytics engineering use cases and SQL. My hypothesis was born of the deluge of Flink SQL and change data capture (CDC) content lately.
To my surprise, Flink's usage is quite diverse!
It's great to see such a well written and fair response. It's also nice to contrast pragmatic and academic perspectives.
For more complex use cases (especially in the data warehouse), data engineers are forced into dbt, Python scripts, templating languages, and custom tools.
Other data warehouse users—analytics engineers—often lack Terraform experience.
Reasoning about time globally is really helpful for distributed systems.
Time Sync now runs with double-digit microsecond accuracy.
Matrix operations are useful—something I wish I knew when taking Linear Algebra in college—particularly for vector search and running LLMs on CPUs.
Nearly every modern video game console since 1998 has incorporated a SIMD processor somewhere in its architecture.
Epoxy is a protocol for providing transactions across heterogeneous data stores.
Unlike X/Open XA, Epoxy provides transactional isolation, not just atomicity.
Epoxy isn't without tradeoffs, though.
More and more people are buying into the idea that S3 is a good storage layer for streaming.
Neon is such an elegant project.
I had assumed this was already available, but it turns out Confluent was keeping it as a paid feature.
Neon takes PostgreSQL and replaces its storage layer with remote storage interfaces.
Unlike SQLite and libsql, SKDB is rebuilt from the ground up to support such use cases.
SKDB is a new kind of database: a reactive edge database that supports materialized views, table subscriptions, and diff'ing.
Personally, I'm speedrunning the hype cycle wave and living in the trough of disillusionment right now.
Express is a helpful building block, but it doesn't change storage architectures much. We still need caches and write-ahead logs (WALs) in front of S3 to reduce cost and keep high availability.
I've been cheerleading S3-as-primary-storage for a while now. It's great to see AWS show some love to this use case.
With r3map, microVMs like Firecracker and gVisor can now support live migrations just like heavier-weight VMs.
ML/AI process migration is of particular interest to me. Microsoft and Facebook have had this technology for a while. GPUs and compute are hard to come by.
We are not experienced cryptographers, and do not see a way to reduce the HighwayHash algorithm to provably secure constructions.
I was surprised to find that there isn't any Prometheus cloud hosting.
Prometheus is intentionally designed with only single-node storage.
Getting started is easy because you don't have to set up a distributed system. Users can defer the scaling decision until later.
This architecture is all about user choice. Kudos to the Prometheus designers.
OLAP DBs should work, but I was really looking for something purpose-built for common metric characteristics.
It's built exactly as I'd hoped—a log-structured merge-tree (LSM). The first level stores Apache Arrow records. Data is then rewritten into Parquet for subsequent levels.
I've been thinking lately about what it means to have all our data directly on S3.
Does it make hybrid transaction/analytical processing (HTAP) and multi-model databases easier to build or more likely to be successful?
PostgreSQL and its extensions continue to be a pragmatic solution to, well, everything.
Analytics Twitter continues to be very—obnoxiously—loud.
The trend toward S3 persistence for streaming captured my interest.
Different databases support different isolation levels, and the same isolation level can mean different things in different databases.
It took one of the greatest software minds of a generation to prove that one of the most popular databases of all time was broken.
Developers that are bitten by database anomalies often don't know.
Applications exhibit some weird anomaly, which developers can't reproduce. Engineers throw up their hands, shrug, and move on. They never even know the database was the culprit.
Jepsen is doing us a favor by showing us that the almighty OLTP database is in fact fallible.
Make sure you understand how your database behaves (as best you can) and act accordingly. Caveat emptor.
Gunnar Morling nerd snipes the internet into building Arrow in Java.
NVMe's are insanely fast—it takes only a couple of seconds to read the 12 gigs of data on my M2.
Most of the focus has been on speeding up the text parsing.
My own implementation clocks in at a measly 53 seconds. I never got around to adding SIMD.
If you attempt this project, beware that hardware is quite variable.
Parca is able to achieve its minimally invasive profiling claims by using an eBPF filter that samples the entire OS's stack at 19hz
FrostDB, an embedded storage engine built on Datafusion and Parquet
L4S is based on the insight that the root cause of queuing delay is in the capacity-seeking congestion controllers of senders, not in the queue itself.
Realtime applications—video and gaming—are an important part of our lives. These applications don't do well when their network path is congested.
Since L4S sits low in the network stack—in both layer 3 (IP) and layer 4 (TCP/UDP) of the OSI model—your code should automatically benefit without any changes.
Zed is a new data model that unifies the JSON and relational models to make data as easy as ever.
I am happy to see that Zed's data model is very similar to Recap's. We both made the same design choices independently. This is validating and bodes well for both projects.
Recap is much slimmer; it deliberately limits itself to reading, writing, and converting between service, event, and database schemas.
SKDB is built as a reactive database that features performant materialized views, diffing between databases, and streaming via ephemeral tables
To an application developer, this feels like your data team is giving you the middle finger.
Engineers discovering their data is consumed downstream is but one gentle example of cross-team coordination complexity.
Throwing more tools at the problem without addressing culture is not going to help.
Application developers want an integrated solution that makes it easy to safely evolve schemas and maintain SLAs.
But the hardest part of data contracts aren't even the tools, it's managing the data culture at organizations.
Neither stream processors nor the outbox pattern do this.
Eighteen years ago, Hadoop broke the data warehouse into compute, data, and control planes—a paradigm that persists to this day.
A specific database might spend the majority of its innovation tokens in just one layer; say, the optimizer.

Unlike the projects above, which focus on a single layer, DataFusion does everything. You can use it as an integrated query engine like Trino or DuckDB, or you can use it as a library for any one of the layers above. It's a complete database toolkit.
Developers are treating PostgreSQL almost like DuckDB—an integrated query engine with a pluggable storage layer.
A world centered around object stores is a serious threat to Apache Kafka.

Kafka's primary use case is data integration—moving data between systems. But if all of your data is on an object store from start (OLTP) to finish (OLAP, search, graph, etc.) what use have you for Kafka?
A world with purpose-built, low latency, high throughput, multi-region, multi-model, transactional databases is coming; it's just a matter of time.
SQLite ignores column types (unless STRICT is used). A value of any type can be written to any column.
If the declared type for a column contains any of the strings 'REAL', 'FLOA', or 'DOUB' then the column has REAL affinity.
APIs and SDKs work better for dynamic execution with branching and loops.
As durable execution systems move up the stack and workflow systems move down the stack, their addressable market (and competitors) will increase.
These different models exist because they are well suited for different users and use cases.
Their Down the Rabbit Hole post is a must-read for anyone fixated on Gunnar Morling's 1brc challenge.
SQL is built for humans. Machines are much better at generating structured messages.
Under the hood, query builders are all generating text SQL right now. I've always found this strange.
ORMs and query builders should be able to go straight to the IR.
Programmatic database access would become a first-class citizen.
It's silly to have applications generate text-based SQL; they should be allowed to pass query plans to the database.
Imagine if PostgreSQL had a from_substrait() extension.
Surprisingly, I haven't found any support for substrait in the OLTP space.
State is the hardest part of stream processing.
Literally every part was pluggable. This was great for LinkedIn because we had proprietary implementations for many of these layers. For open source users it was a rats nest of configuration.
We preferred smaller, purpose-built systems rather than one giant monolithic platform.
As for Flink, I have to ask, what is Flink for? The answer seems to be, 'It's for everything!'
Flink is just buzzier.
Privately, Flink developers admit to me that even they think it's too complicated.

You could interpret Confluent's Immerok acquisition as a capitulation to monolithic platform architecture. Or you could interpret it as a sign that customers with deep pockets fell into the one-platform trap and now have to pay someone for help.
I believe they will be as impactful as Hadoop was a decade ago.
Frankly, I see little reason to use Velox.
DataFusion is a superset of Velox's functionality.
ElectricSQL with PostgreSQL on both the client and server simplifies a lot.
This ended up being a bad idea for a variety of reasons; it caused performance and ETL problems.
Non-time-ordered UUIDs values generated in succession are not sequential. The randomly generated values will not be clustered to each other in a database index.
I'm excited about UUID v7.
If every database is a PostgreSQL-compatible frontend built atop the same set of open source components, how are these databases going to compete?
Multi-model databases fundamentally change application architectures for the better.
A world with a bunch of purpose-built PostgreSQL compatible databases is only incrementally better. Developers must still adopt a different database and vendor for each access pattern.

Many developers are skeptical of HTAP databases; the industry is littered with failures. Multi-model systems look even more challenging; they try to collapse yet more access patterns. This is a feature, not a bug, though.
There's no winner for this type of database yet, so there's room to compete. And users do want this, they just can't get it (yet).
Even if every platform uses the same database, there is ample space to compete on the surrounding ecosystem.
Once software is deterministic it's much easier to test. And a test that fails once will always fail—no more flaky tests!
Antithesis solved this problem by writing a custom hypervisor that emulates an entire machine. Software no longer needs to be deterministic in order to do deterministic simulation testing.
When failures happen, Antithesis can play back every instruction—not just the application instructions, but all of the operating system ones as well.

Developers use distributed call tracing to figure out which microservices are causing the most latency for the frontend call. Once the bottleneck is found, a different set of tools—profilers—is used to figure out why the service is slow.
An object store with such characteristics will completely change how we build data systems.
The hard parts of distributed data infrastructure will be managed by the object store.

Graphs can be thought of as nearest-neighbor search where edges define explicit links to nearest neighbors. Conversely, nearest neighbor search can be thought of as graph traversal, where nearest neighbors define implicit edges in the graph.

So much of web and mobile development is about ferrying data back and forth and coding across the network that the choice tends to dominate the architecture of your app and the code you have to write.
We see local-first as the best way of building pretty much all software in the future.
The delight and the promise of local-first is to be able to reduce the complexity of the development stack to just your data and your components.

Writes made to the local embedded database in the client are not subject to being rejected by the server. This gets rid of rollbacks and tentativity, which is a key advance in simplifying the local-first programming model.
Whilst the software architecture orthodoxy emphasizes the cons of tight coupling, there genuinely are both pros and cons here.
If you're building consumer, prosumer, or SaaS software and you're not building local-first, ask yourself how your product is going to compare to a product that is.
Do you want your product to feel old and laggy like Jira or super snappy and modern, like Linear and Superhuman?
Kafka is now a protocol, not an Apache project. The Apache project is just one (aging) implementation.
Confluent Cloud's feature set has so diverged from Apache Kafka that it's nearly unrecognizable.
Successful infrastructure is destined to be a protocol.
I don't see a pure open source business—à la Hortonworks—as a viable model.
Users are going to have to get comfortable inferring a company's intentions based on their actions.
All of this begs the question: what responsibility—if any—do companies have to their open source roots, or to the protocol itself?
They worry—rightly so—that they'll alienate users and suffer brand damage if they explicitly abandon their open source roots. So they opt for strategic ambiguity.
I'm really annoyed that you all are making me.
How do you know if someone writes Rust? They will tell you.
Rust is the opposite of what I want; it's a thick language with a thin standard library.
People have been making jokes about node_modules for a decade now, but this problem is just as bad in Rust codebases I've seen.
Focus on debugging your application rather than debugging your programming language knowledge.
Ecosystems are more important than languages to me.
A platform is a coherent and cohesive set of features that seamlessly work together to provide a service more than the sum of its parts.
Tying yourself to PostgreSQL is always going to be more limiting than building your own database. You're limited by what PostgreSQL can offer you.
The more control you want, the more PostgreSQL is going to feel like a straight jacket.
Building your own database doesn't sound as crazy as it used to.
The question is not whether to package or fork PostgreSQL, but whether to extend PostgreSQL using the extensions API or the protocol.
My gut says that PostgreSQL's protocol, not its query engine, will be its most defining characteristic.
Multi-model databases amortize the adoption cost across multiple use cases.
New infrastructure must justify its upfront adoption cost.
I believe that the future of database persistence is object storage—S3, Google Cloud Storage, and so on.
I don't think we'll need these write caches in the future. Object storage is changing fast.
A high-volume service that sustains 10,000 writes per-second would cost 2.5c per-second, or $65,000 per-month.
Batching decouples the client write-rate from the PUT calls sent to object storage.
This triad of latency, cost, and durability presents an easy mental model; something I'm facetiously calling the latency, cost, durability (LCD) theorem.
Clients that want low latency and high durability have to pay.
LCD-style systems have a rather elegant design.
Infrastructure developers could just get over themselves.
Reference counting provides deterministic performance in most cases, but that performance can still be complex to analyze and predict.
For current Rust users, if Rust kicks the bucket, Swift is a good plan B.
I'm torn on these changes. On the one hand, I like Swift's relatively simple memory management. On the other hand, I buy the argument that ARC can be slow in certain circumstances.
Some of ARC's edge cases happen to matter a lot when building deep infrastructure—the kind of stuff you build with Rust.
Perhaps Swift has managed to thread the needle with their ownership semantics in a way that Rust hasn't.
Engineers love to make things extensible. It's an escape hatch for bad decisions, the promise of flexibility, and an openness to new ideas. But extensibility causes fragmentation.
They're dreaming. It's not written in Rust, so that's going to happen. There will be others, too. Engineers just can't resist reimplementing specs in their native languages.
LV2 is so thin, the whole spec is a mere ~200 line Protobuf file.
And we're just talking about encodings. LV2's choice to ditch both encodings and type systems really freaked me out.
I've since realized that LV2 has adopted an unstated philosophy: Arrow will dictate the standard.
This is hell for data engineers. At least, that was my first reaction.
Given all this, it's obvious that the formats are not ready for prime-time yet. Most analytical use cases are still best suited for Parquet.
I'm squarely in the trough of disillusionment with S3.
S3 is the only object store that doesn't support preconditions.
Building a two-phase write between DynamoDB and S3 is not technically difficult, but it's annoying and leads to ugly abstractions.
S3E1Z looks more like an expensive EBS with a half-implemented S3 API.
The dream is that developers are offered an object store with all of these features: low latency, preconditions, dual-region/multi-region, and so on. But we live in reality.
The path to the slope of enlightenment is to recognize that S3 is an object store not a file system.
It's important to talk about a company's failures as well as their successes.
Nowhere does Google's post even apologize. In fact, the only apology I can find is on UniSuper's website.
While this may be true, I find it completely tone-deaf. So much for customer empathy.
Enterprises expect more than a terse single-page remediation with no apology.
You don't want to A/B test user interfaces in an organization when they're in the middle of a production outage.
Google could have audited their other deletion flows, their other tools, and their other products. They could have modified deletion workflows to stage deletions. Instead, they did none of this. Why?
Google's remediation indicates that they don't understand how damaging this outage was to their brand.
If Google won't use their cloud service, what business do you have selling it to others?
Antithesis's design is ground breaking. Developers can now take any containerized code and run it deterministically on Antithesis. DST for the masses.
There's no doubt in my mind that their product is worth well north of $200,000 for a large company selling infrastructure to hundreds of customers. For early stage startups, it's a tough sell.
Building a non-deterministic hypervisor is just too juicy of a problem. There will be open source implementations.
Yes, it's a novel innovation, but it seems inevitable that there will be open source implementations.
Most of Antithesis's value isn't in their hypervisor anyway.
I'm very excited to hear it's on their minds, and they're planning to open up their hypervisor.
pg_lakehouse is proof that composable data systems are not only possible, but are already here.
Systems have changed but the pattern remains the same.
What I want is a cache that sits in front of warehouse data and exposes it through a low-latency API.
The 'Connect-as-a-Service' part of Connect was completely absent.
We had to dumb-down Connect's cluster management features and bypass the HTTP API in order to achieve our goals.
I do think it's healthy for competitors like Bento and Redpanda Connect to present alternatives.
Table formats like Iceberg, Hudi, and Delta always struck me as an odd layer for companies to fight over.
The formats themselves are specifications that define a file, folder, and byte structure in a filesystem. It felt akin to building companies around storage formats like Parquet or ORC.
The layer above the table format—the catalog—is the more important layer; it's where query engines integrate.
An integrated catalog, table format, and file format is compelling. Such a product contains the entry point to the data plane and much of the data plane itself.
A dominant catalog company could represent a significant threat if the company chose to move up the stack by offering or even favoring a query engine.
Another vendor taking control of the data layer could be quite disruptive. So we see vendors moving down the stack to shore up their product offerings.
Yes, but have you looked at the code?
Out of all of these announcements, all this marketing, and all this noise, we've gotten one partial re-write, one empty Github project, and one hello world Rust file.
It appears the vendors have gotten ahead of themselves.
I'm not even sure they all understand why they're open sourcing these projects.
I'd love to see someone write down their data lakehouse strategy, or at least do a better job of communicating what the end state of all this is.
Right now it looks like flailing.
It's often hard to extricate internal projects. A rewrite is often the right move.
I would love to see everyone congregate around Apache Iceberg's REST API for core lakehouse catalog functionality.
I'd like to see query engines take a more active role in evolving the spec, particularly Apache DataFusion and Trino.
Reorienting the API evolution around the query engines it serves means more projects can take an active role, thereby reducing the control of one or two vendors.
Don't mistake 'non-core' for not valuable. Much of the functionality in this bucket is extremely valuable, if not required for enterprises.
Data lakehouse vendors have accidentally stumbled upon the wedge that traditional data catalogs have been struggling to find.
Traditional data catalogs have been unable to shift from nice-to-have to must-have in the enterprise; they remain a dusty tool that's often ignored once installed.
Data lakehouse catalogs are must-have for anyone running query engines on a data lake.
Unless you are a vertical cloud, you probably only need to design for your largest customers.
As we look at the next 5 years, customer data is only going to increase in value.
It feels like each B2B/infrastructure company has invested all this money, time, and energy into building SaaS offerings; yet their customer's do not actually want them. It just feels… broken.
Instead of building a sophisticated SaaS multi-tenant offering that many customers don't actually want, you can focus all that energy on just building a great BYOC experience.
Imagine a world where all SaaS companies are running in their customer's cloud account. SaaS margins would be purely R&D and support.
All of today's cloud products are meant for building software, not consuming software.
We believe in a world where most software is run in customer accounts.
It was a classic vanity metric. Naturally, the product was popular and drove considerable subscriber revenue.
OLAP developed many fancy concepts—cubes, dimensions, measures, and so on. But it's really just SELECT COUNT(*) FROM … GROUP BY queries.

Data warehouses are built to handle queries on large data sets but with slower response times (seconds to minutes). WVMP requires millisecond response times since end-users are waiting for the page to load.
A single profile view resulted in one write (or increment) for each combination of dimensions—high write amplification.
Druid had a clever solution to the batch-write problem: store recently ingested in-memory as well.
Data could be ingested through Kafka topics in realtime because writes simply went to memory. Ingested data was immediately available for reads.
The history of the technology industry is a story of turning lower layers of the application stack into commoditized building blocks.
Like the cloud infrastructure example, lower level plumbing can be slow to build, expensive, hard to maintain and get right, and often offers little differentiated business value.
Don't fight physics; minimize data transit.
The working set of data queried day-to-day is normally 2-3 orders of magnitude smaller than the data the application could query.
Spice inverts the application-data warehouse relationship to be more distributed.
Separation from the language and runtime enables common functionality to be portable and platform agnostic, while maintaining performance through a very fast kernel-level local loopback.
As you move up this stack, you want to focus on delivering user value at higher and higher levels, not deal with the lower level data plumbing.
Fraudsters act quickly and often use scripts. We needed activity data to be available to the model on the order of seconds so we could detect fraudulent behavior as it was occurring.
I started calling the former 'human interaction' and the latter 'machine interaction'.

Human interaction OLAP with realtime slice-and-dice dashboards. Response times could take a few hundred milliseconds while the chart updated. Machine interaction, by contrast, had pre-defined queries but required much faster response times.
I started to see realtime OLAP everywhere.
The Kibana dashboards looked an awful lot like business analytics chart'n'graph dashboards to me. The data was just machine instances, CPUs, and disks.
Data warehouses, real-time OLAP systems, time series databases, and search engines might all converge.

We had a tight bound on the number of milliseconds that a transaction would wait for a fraud response. If the window passed, we had to decide whether to reject the transaction outright or risk allowing fraudulent transactions through.
Object storage allows SlateDB to provide bottomless storage capacity, high durability, and easy replication.
We built SlateDB because we felt an open source LSM on object storage would be useful for stateful stream processing, serverless functions, durable execution, and more.
Companies like WarpStream and Turbopuffer set zero disk architecture on the map.
We couldn't find an LSM that was purpose-built for object storage. So we built it.
I am explicitly not going to do anything with SlateDB other than help make it a successful open source project.
I am not going to start a company around it or try and monetize it in any way.
They have the mentality of a team on a budget. Like they didn't have 1000s of machines to throw at the problem. They had to make it work on what they had.
They've occupied a really nice spot between embedded OLAP systems like DuckDB and distributed realtime OLAP systems like Apache Pinot and Apache Druid.
It's surprising to me that there aren't more single-process realtime OLAP systems out there; it seems obvious in hindsight.
A great DX is nice and all, but does it scale and is it easy to operate?
The operational flexibility to scale up or out without adding a bunch of services is really valuable.
Stories like these are somewhat alarming for large-scale enterprises.
If PostgreSQL takes ClickHouse's single-node and small-scale usage, and systems like Pinot and Druid take its large-scale market, there's not much left.
In my view Postgres isn't having a renaissance but is rather 'coming of age'. The last 30 years of smart design decisions have slowly made it the best open-source RDBMS.
Extensions enable Postgres to stay current with innovations in the data space while also maintaining extreme care to keep core as small and robust as possible.
We believe customers should pick a product by thinking about their business need(s) rather than about the infrastructure of the product itself.
Building analytics in Postgres is really hard. Doing it properly is much harder than what ParadeDB could do today.
I am worried that MotherDuck, who seems to be the primary company driving this, does not care enough about Postgres to really make the project what it needs to be.
Hacking on Postgres is really hard. It's hard to find developers who know Postgres core.
The clear separation of work granted by extensions allow Postgres to uniquely accomplish the best of both worlds: Stay relevant while being rock-solid.
You have a fever, and the only prescription is more microservices.
I have microservice fatigue.
Yes, writing modular code is a good idea, and yes it's obvious. Yet, we're not doing it.
Rather than fixing the monolithic code, we've jumped straight to microservices. Why? I think the answer is tooling.
In practice microservices can be just as tough to wrangle as monoliths.
If a developer breaks the monolith, they've broken it for everyone.
We've spent decades building tooling for microservices. It's time to give monoliths the same respect.
When there's a tradeoff between quality and time to market, time to market almost always takes precedence.
After leaving Google, I noticed a gap in the broader industry, where microservices and NoSQL databases were often adopted without a solid theoretical foundation.

The main reason was the complexity of TLA+'s syntax and semantics. Its syntax isn't intuitive—it relies heavily on ASCII representations of mathematical symbols, which can feel alien to most programmers.
The time taken to model a system in FizzBee is often less than the time it takes to write a traditional design document.
Fixing them with code changes like retries or locks didn't address the root cause—it often just moved the problem elsewhere.
As AI-assisted coding becomes more prevalent, validating that the code performs as intended will be essential for AI adoption.
Acquisition motivation and communication are rarely the same.
There's no one-size-fits-all deployment offering.
While there's no one-size-fits-all deployment offering, I suspect we can still build a single system that can accommodate flexible deployment.
I really didn't understand why everyone was so excited about ClickHouse until I dug into it. Then it clicked: you can run ClickHouse as a single node.
In hindsight, it's a comically obvious gap in the realtime online analytical processing (OLAP) space.
It scales from a single in-process library all the way to a distributed cluster. This is the future.

Infrastructure vendors need to ask themselves: does our system deploy self-managed, BYOC, and as a SaaS cloud? Can we support in-process embedded, single-node, and clustered execution? If not, you're cooked.
For many smaller organizations, these tools are overkill.
Developers are thinking: if I can get by with a small query engine (DuckDB), maybe I can get by with a small ETL tool.
These days, we're asking ML, AI, analytics, and product engineers to build data pipelines.
It ran at 15m intervals and it was remarkably effective.
Data consumers want control of their data pipelines.
If everyone is managing their own data pipelines, there will be duplication.
The pipelines could become a tangled mess with no clear ownership as employees come and go.
Until recently, these technologies have offered fairly generic features. Consequently, they kind of work for many use cases, but truly work for relatively few.
Moving your workload to a different machine (or cloud) could save money, unlock better GPUs, or speed up training and inference.
As developers execute operations on a GPU, the GPU's memory accumulates state. This GPU data is very difficult to read directly, which poses a problem if you wish to snapshot a machine's state.
Architect purports to migrate faster than a spot instance preemption occurs, which would allow for huge cost savings for many workloads.
We will, for example, see more multi-cloud adoption as state is easier to migrate and GPUs are harder to come by.
Serverless functions might supplant service oriented applications, too.
DuckDB is essentially SQLite for columnar data.
It's untenable to install DuckDB on everyone's laptop, grant everyone access to data lake buckets, and ask them to run queries from the CLI.
What they don't say is that the most valuable queries in an organization are large: financial reconciliation, recommendation systems, advertising, and others. These are the revenue drivers.
They might comprise a minority of all the queries an organization runs, but they make the money.
As much as MotherDuck would like to be the DuckDB vendor, they're a cloud data warehouse that just happens to use DuckDB.
It's often easier to cut costs in your existing data warehouse by auditing queries and data retention.
MotherDuck has picked a fight with some of the nastiest apex predators out there: Snowflake, BigQuery, and Databricks.
It's amazing middleware, much like SQLite. I don't see it as a data warehouse, though.
I've long held that creating the 'analytics engineer' role was a mistake.
I don't believe that this set of activities is enough value to justify a full headcount; it's too limited in scope and too far removed from revenue generation.
Dumping garbage into an object store made it easy for data engineers to ignore transformations and gave analytics engineers something to do.
dbtLabs, Motherduck, and other MDS vendors were all too willing to create a new role to sell their products, which dovetailed nicely with analyst's desire to be engineers and get paid more.
LLMs are replacing analysts in some cases. Screech all you want, but it's happening.
It's time to merge data engineers and analytics engineers back into a single data team that's responsible for E, T, and L.
I expect to see a collapse of data engineering and analytics engineering back to a single team in the next few years.
You know, we wouldn't need any of this if we had a distributed filesystem that worked.
A scalable remote filesystem with normal POSIX semantics would let us build distributed systems that were stateless services; we could use the distributed filesystem to store everything.
Object stores are still missing POSIX semantics, though. You can't take any old system that uses filesystem I/O libraries and use S3 as its storage layer.

Direct object storage integration makes sense for systems like databases, whose primary job is to store and query data. But for systems, frameworks, and libraries that just need to read and write files as part of a broader workload, rewriting the storage layer for object storage is too burdensome.
Kubernetes has made every system distributed.
I think we're finally getting to the point where we have both the technology and the demand to get us what we need: a distributed POSIX-compatible filesystem.
I hit the roof: our data pipeline (and team) wasn't built for this.
Despite all this demand, 'reporting', as we called it, was rarely invested in.
Customers want data from their SaaS vendors. Yet, for the past decade (longer, really), SaaS vendors have been left to roll their own solutions.

Offering customers data in a data lake format maintains some of the benefits that SFTP has historically provided: data transfers are very fast, access can be managed centrally in the catalog, and files can be atomically exposed to customers.
Customer data lakes might actually end up being a driving force for lakehouse adoption.
Giving a customer's analysts DuckDB and pointing them at an S3 bucket is pretty compelling.
These lakehouses could also give analytics engineers something to do—something that drives revenue.
I believe Confluent didn't build WarpStream because it wasn't set up, as an organization, to build such a product.

WarpStream was in the process of building a cheaper (and arguably better) version of Confluent's platform with a handful of engineers that could service the majority of Confluent customers' use cases. That's a pretty staggering statement.

Why would Confluent build Freight without BYOC support? I think the answer is that it was too encumbered by its legacy tech stack, its customers (the innovator's dilemma), and its organization (Conway's law).

If Confluent could service the majority of their users with a product that looked like WarpStream, what happens to the Kora team, the legacy Kafka teams, the entire sales motion, the marketing, and so on? Such a pivot would be seismic.
It's simply much easier to build very lean, very efficient companies now.
Legacy companies built their teams before the AI shift happened. It's harder and more painful to turn the ship.
Kafka, itself, is commoditized.
Legacy infrastructure companies are going to have a tough time competing with these new cloud native, AI-enabled, post-ZIRP companies.

We've seen companies make customer-facing analytics 100x faster, drop infrastructure costs by an order of magnitude, and turn reports that took hours to execute into ones that complete in under a second.

Adopting new database technologies is a pretty scary endeavor. We found that asking companies to install a new extension (that could potentially crash) on their production database was a pretty big ask to make.

I believe IVMs offer a pretty fundamental change in how organizations can work with their data—building data structures around specific queries vs. trying to store data to be 'generically' fast for any query.

I began to realize that these systems were all data catalogs. Some called themselves registries, others were information_schema, and many called themselves catalogs. But they were all handling metadata about your data; they seemed 80% the same.
I'm not convinced that each of these use cases deserves its own distinct catalog. Each use case has separate requirements, but it seems to me that one system could service them all.
We spent the last 10-15 years doing data integration. Data pipelines are supply chains now.
All of our data (and metadata) flows through OLTP, streaming systems, batch, and data warehouse systems. It no longer makes sense to have a separate catalog for each use case.
The optimist in me hopes the convergence continues; 'data catalog' should mean only one thing.

If Iceberg's REST API evolves fast enough, it might become the lingua franca that we need. The realist in me knows that this is unlikely. Instead, we'll probably end up with catalog gateways or proxies.

A better experience increases the adoption cost for new software (or decreases the cost of using legacy software).
A partnership between Windsurf and a company bringing a new software product to market would be symbiotic.

I like to think of timely dataflow a lot like Hadoop's Map/Reduce: a powerful, but low-level framework for data processing.
DBSP can convert any batch-based SQL to an incremental query.

The progression from timely dataflow to differential dataflow and then to DBSP shows a clear trajectory: moving from highly flexible, low-level frameworks toward more structured, to easier-to-use incremental computation systems.
Differential dataflow is just really complicated.

Much of the story of the past 10 years has been about adoption rather than innovation.

ZeroFS provides network filesystem (NFS), network block device (NBD), and Plan 9 Filesystem Protocol (9P) implementations on top of SlateDB.
SlateDB's branching and forking features mean ZeroFS will be able to provide zero-copy filesystem forking—an important feature for AI and many other use cases.

This begs the question: why do we need MCP for tool definitions? We already have OpenAPI, gRPC, and CLIs.
ChatGPT is actually better at CLIs than most humans—just watch Codex fly through sed, awk, and grep commands.
Viewed from this lens, MCP seems like a temporary kludge.
The argument seems to be that the same people that wrote the bad OpenAPI specs are going to write good MCP specs. I don't buy it.
There's no law that LLMs need a new protocol to interact with software.
The protocol is just plumbing. What matters is whether your tool helps or hinders the agent's ability to complete tasks.
MCP is a victim of its own success.
